title: Rigid Graph Products
overall_summary: The paper develops deformation-rigidity tools for von Neumann algebraic graph products, focusing on relative amenability, Popa intertwining, quasi-normalizers, primeness, free-product decompositions, strong solidity, and unique rigid graph product decompositions. The techniques repeatedly exploit the standard link decomposition M_Γ = M_Star(v) ∗_{M_Link(v)} M_{Γ\{v}}, precise conditional-expectation calculations for graph-product reduced words, and Popa-Vaes normalizer dichotomies for amalgamated free products. For the original problem, the most directly relevant part is Section 8: under finite irreducibility, at least three vertices, and trace-zero vertex unitaries, the paper proves that the graph product is a prime II_1 factor and establishes the key non-intertwining result M_Γ not≺ M_Λ for every proper subgraph Λ. The relative amenability intersection theorem and quasi-normalizer localization results are also potentially useful for proving proper proximality, because they turn amenability relative to several subgraph algebras into amenability relative to their intersection and control possible join/tensor obstructions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.1 and Lemma 2.2, Popa intertwining notation and transitivity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,τ) be a finite von Neumann algebra, and let A⊆1_A M 1_A and B⊆1_B M 1_B be von Neumann subalgebras. The notation A≺_M B means Popa intertwining: equivalently, there exist projections p∈A and q∈B, a normal *-homomorphism θ:pAp→qBq, and a nonzero partial isometry v∈qMp such that θ(x)v=vx for all x∈pAp; equivalently, there is no net of unitaries u_i∈A such that ||E_B(x* u_i y)||_2→0 for every x,y∈1_A M 1_B; equivalently, L^2(M) contains a nonzero A-B subbimodule of finite right B-dimension. The notation A≺^s_M B means Ar≺_M B for every projection r∈A′∩M. Lemma 2.2 states: if P≺_M Q and Q≺^s_M R, then P≺_M R; and if Pz≺_M Q for every nonzero projection z in Nor_{1_P M 1_P}(P)′∩1_P M 1_P, then P≺^s_M Q. In particular, if Q′∩1_Q M 1_Q is a factor and P≺_M Q and Q≺_M R, then P≺_M R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalences defining A≺_M B are quoted from Popa's intertwining-by-bimodules theory. The transitivity statement is quoted from earlier work of Drimbe-Hoff-Ioana and Vaes. The key mechanism is that finite-index bimodules compose under relative tensor product, while the stable intertwining hypothesis prevents loss of support when passing through corners. The final factor-relative-commutant observation follows because in a factor relative commutant a nonzero intertwining corner can be upgraded to the stable form needed for transitivity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.6, link amalgamated free product decomposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, and for each vertex v∈Γ let (M_v,φ_v) be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful state. For a subgraph Λ⊆Γ write M_Λ for the graph product over Λ. If v∈Γ, put Γ_1=Star_Γ(v)={v}∪Link_Γ(v), Λ=Link_Γ(v), and Γ_2=Γ\{v}, and assume Γ_2 is nonempty. Then the graph product von Neumann algebra decomposes as a reduced amalgamated free product M_Γ = M_{Γ_1} ∗_{M_Λ} M_{Γ_2}, with respect to the canonical trace- or state-preserving conditional expectations onto M_Λ. The analogous statement holds for reduced C^*-algebraic graph products A_Γ = A_{Γ_1} ∗_{A_Λ} A_{Γ_2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is recalled from Caspers-Fima. The graph-product relations split the vertices into the star of v and the complement Γ\{v}; the common relations between the two sides are exactly those through Link(v). Reduced graph-product words alternating between letters outside the link on the two sides are orthogonal to M_Link(v), matching the reduced amalgamated free product Fock-space construction. The canonical conditional expectations onto subgraph algebras agree with the amalgamating expectations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.1, iterated conditional expectations in graph products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph with subgraphs Γ_1,Γ_2⊆Γ. Let (M_Γ,φ_Γ)=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,φ_v) be a graph product of von Neumann algebras with normal faithful states, and write E_{M_Λ}:M_Γ→M_Λ for the canonical conditional expectation onto a subgraph Λ⊆Γ. Let W_Γ be the right-angled Coxeter group of Γ, used to index reduced word types, and for a word s∈W_Γ let Link_Γ(s) be the intersection of the links of its letters. Suppose u,v∈W_Γ admit reduced decompositions u=u_l u_c u_r and v=v_l v_c v_r with u_l,v_l∈W_{Γ_1}, u_r,v_r∈W_{Γ_2}, and with u_c,v_c not starting with letters from Γ_1 and not ending with letters from Γ_2. If a=a_l a_c a_r and b=b_l b_c b_r are reduced operators with a_l∈˚M_{u_l}, a_c∈˚M_{u_c}, a_r∈˚M_{u_r}, b_l∈˚M_{v_l}, b_c∈˚M_{v_c}, and b_r∈˚M_{v_r}, then for every x∈M_Γ one has E_{M_{Γ_2}}(a* E_{M_{Γ_1}}(x) b) = φ_Γ(a_c* b_c) a_r* E_{M_{Γ_1∩Γ_2∩Link(u_c)}}(a_l* x b_l) b_r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First pull out the left and right parts lying in the target subgraph algebras: since a_l,b_l∈M_{Γ_1} and a_r,b_r∈M_{Γ_2}, the expression reduces to a_r* E_{M_{Γ_2}}(a_c* E_{M_{Γ_1}}(a_l* x b_l) b_c) b_r. The remaining central calculation is exactly the graph-product conditional-expectation computation from Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson: the only part of E_{M_{Γ_1}}(a_l* x b_l) surviving multiplication by a_c*,b_c and projection to M_{Γ_2} is the component supported in Γ_1∩Γ_2∩Link(u_c), and the middle reduced-word overlap contributes the scalar φ_Γ(a_c* b_c). Combining the two reductions gives the displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.3, relative amenability intersects over subgraph algebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph with subgraphs Γ_1,Γ_2⊆Γ. For each vertex v∈Γ let (M_v,τ_v) be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful trace, and let M_Γ=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,τ_v). Let P⊆M_Γ be a von Neumann subalgebra. If P is amenable relative to M_{Γ_1} inside M_Γ and also amenable relative to M_{Γ_2} inside M_Γ, then P is amenable relative to M_{Γ_1∩Γ_2} inside M_Γ. Here relative amenability means that there is a P-central positive functional on the Jones basic construction 1_P⟨M_Γ,e_{M_{Γ_i}}⟩1_P restricting to the trace on 1_P M_Γ 1_P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After reducing to unital inclusions, set Q_i=M_{Γ_i} and Q_0=Q_1∩Q_2=M_{Γ_1∩Γ_2}. Consider the Connes relative tensor product H=L^2(⟨M_Γ,e_{Q_1}⟩)⊗_{M_Γ} L^2(⟨M_Γ,e_{Q_2}⟩). The assumptions imply, by the standard characterization of relative amenability through weak containment of correspondences, that the M_Γ-P correspondence L^2(M_Γ) is weakly contained in H. A dense subspace of H is spanned by vectors x e_{Q_1} y ⊗_{M_Γ} e_{Q_2} z, where y is a reduced word not starting in Γ_1 and not ending in Γ_2. Proposition 5.1 gives the inner product identity E_{Q_0}(y* E_{Q_0}(x* x′) y′)=E_{Q_1}(y* E_{Q_2}(x* x′) y′), which identifies this dense subspace isometrically with a subspace of a correspondence built from L^2(M_Γ)⊗_{Q_0}L^2(M_Γ). Thus H is weakly contained in the correspondence witnessing relative amenability over Q_0, and P is amenable relative to M_{Γ_1∩Γ_2}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.8, quasi-normalizer localization from minimal subgraph intertwining
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, and for each v∈Γ let (M_v,τ_v) be a tracial von Neumann algebra. Let Λ⊆Γ be a subgraph and let {Λ_j}_{j∈J} be a nonempty family of subgraphs of Γ. Define Λ_emb = Λ ∪ ⋂_{j∈J} ⋃_{v∈Λ\Λ_j} Link_Γ(v). Let A⊆1_A M_Γ 1_A be a von Neumann subalgebra. First, if A⊆1_A M_Λ 1_A and A does not intertwine into M_{Λ_j} inside M_Γ for any j∈J, then: (a) there is a net of unitaries u_i∈A such that ||E_{M_{Λ_emb}}(a u_i b)||_2→0 for all a,b∈1_A M_Γ 1_A with E_{M_{Λ_emb}}(a)=E_{M_{Λ_emb}}(b)=0; (b) 1_A qNor_{M_Γ}(A)′′ 1_A⊆M_{Λ_emb}; and (c) if u∈M_Γ is a unitary with u* A u⊆M_{Λ_emb}, then 1_A u 1_A∈M_{Λ_emb}. Second, if P=Nor_{M_Γ}(A)′′ and r∈P∩P′ is a projection such that rA≺_{M_Γ} M_Λ but rA does not intertwine into any M_{Λ_j}, then rP≺_{M_Γ} M_{Λ_emb}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), Lemma 5.5 supplies a single net of unitaries u_i∈A that is asymptotically orthogonal to every M_{Λ_j}. Take reduced words b,c orthogonal to M_{Λ_emb}, decompose their word types relative to Λ_emb and Λ, and apply Proposition 5.1. Because the central word part contains a letter outside Λ_emb, one can choose j such that Λ∩Link(central word)⊆Λ_j. The resulting norm is bounded by a scalar multiple of ||E_{M_{Λ_j}}(b_l* u_i c_l)||_2, which tends to zero. Density extends this to all a,b orthogonal to M_{Λ_emb}. For (b) and (c), the mixing net from (a) is fed into Vaes's quasi-normalizer lemma: if Ax is finitely generated as a right A-module, or if Au⊆uM_{Λ_emb}, then the corner 1_A x 1_A, respectively 1_A u 1_A, must lie in M_{Λ_emb}. For the second part, use Popa intertwining to choose a partial isometry v and homomorphism θ:pAp→qM_Λq with θ(pAp) still avoiding all M_{Λ_j}. Applying the first part to θ(pAp) localizes its quasi-normalizer in M_{Λ_emb}. Cutting by central supports and conjugating normalizers of A through v yields an intertwining of rP into M_{Λ_emb}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.9 and Remark 5.10, diffuse vertex subalgebras cannot intertwine into subgraphs omitting the vertex
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, and for each v∈Γ let (M_v,τ_v) be a tracial von Neumann algebra. Fix a vertex v∈Γ, let N⊆M_v be a diffuse von Neumann subalgebra, and let Λ⊆Γ be a subgraph. If N≺_{M_Γ} M_Λ, then v∈Λ. Consequently, when all vertex algebras M_v are II_1 factors and Λ⊆Γ is finite, qNor_{M_Γ}(M_Λ)′′ = Nor_{M_Γ}(M_Λ)′′ = M_{Λ∪Link_Γ(Λ)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If v∉Λ, choose a net of unitaries u_k∈N with τ_v(u_k)=0 and u_k→0 weakly. For reduced words x,y∈M_Γ, write x=x′a and y=by′ with a,b∈M_v and with x′ not ending in v and y′ not starting in v. Split a u_k b into its scalar part τ_v(a u_k b) and its trace-zero part. The scalar coefficient tends to zero by weak convergence. The trace-zero part remains a reduced word containing the vertex v after commuting past link letters, hence its conditional expectation onto M_Λ is zero because v∉Λ. Thus ||E_{M_Λ}(x u_k y)||_2→0 for all reduced x,y and hence for all x,y by density, so Popa's criterion gives N not≺_{M_Γ}M_Λ. For the normalizer formula, M_{Λ∪Link(Λ)} plainly normalizes M_Λ; the reverse inclusion follows from Proposition 5.8 applied to all proper subgraphs of Λ, using Proposition 5.9 to see that M_Λ does not intertwine into any proper subgraph algebra.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.11, unitary conjugacy into the localization subgraph
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, and for each v∈Γ let (M_v,τ_v) be a tracial II_1 factor. Let Q⊆M_Γ be a subfactor such that Q′∩M_Γ is also a factor. Let Λ⊆Γ be a subgraph and let {Λ_j}_{j∈J} be a nonempty collection of subgraphs of Λ. Define Λ_emb = Λ ∪ ⋂_{j∈J} ⋃_{v∈Λ\Λ_j} Link_Γ(v). If Q≺_{M_Γ}M_Λ and Q does not intertwine into M_{Λ_j} for any j∈J, then there is a unitary u∈M_Γ such that u* Q u⊆M_{Λ_emb}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from an intertwining Q≺M_Λ that is minimal relative to the Λ_j. Lemma 5.4 gives projections q∈Q and e∈M_Λ, a normal homomorphism θ:qQq→eM_Λe, and a nonzero partial isometry v∈eM_Γq with θ(x)v=vx, while θ(qQq) still avoids all M_{Λ_j}. Cut q to a subprojection q_0 of rational trace 1/m so that v_0=vq_0 remains nonzero. Proposition 5.8 localizes θ(q_0Qq_0)'s normalizer in M_{Λ_emb}. Since Q is a factor, choose partial isometries in Q whose initial projections are q_0 and whose final projections sum to 1; these produce a nonzero projection q′∈Q′∩M_Γ from v_0* v_0. Because Q′∩M_Γ is a factor, cut q′ to rational trace 1/n and choose partial isometries in Q′∩M_Γ summing to 1. The resulting smaller intertwiner v_{00} has range projection v_{00}v_{00}* lying in M_{Λ_emb}. Since M_{Λ_emb} is a factor, choose partial isometries w_{j,k}∈M_{Λ_emb} with common range v_{00}v_{00}* and initial projections summing to 1. The explicit sum u=Σ_{j,k} u_j u′_k v_{00}* w_{j,k} is unitary, and a matrix-unit calculation gives u*Q u⊆M_{Λ_emb}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.3, Vaes normalizer dichotomy for amalgamated free products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (N_1,τ_1) and (N_2,τ_2) be tracial von Neumann algebras with a common von Neumann subalgebra B⊆N_i such that τ_1|_B=τ_2|_B, and let N=N_1∗_B N_2 be their tracial amalgamated free product. Let A⊆1_A N 1_A be a von Neumann subalgebra that is amenable relative to N_1 or to N_2 inside N. Put P=Nor_{1_A N 1_A}(A)′′. Then at least one of the following holds: A≺_N B; P≺_N N_i for some i∈{1,2}; or P is amenable relative to B inside N.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is quoted from Vaes and is not proved in the paper. In the paper it is applied to the link decomposition M_Γ=M_Star(v)∗_{M_Link(v)}M_{Γ\{v}}. The trichotomy says that an amenable subalgebra of an amalgamated free product must either intertwine into the amalgam, have its normalizer intertwine into one free-product side, or have its normalizer remain amenable relative to the amalgam.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.8, amenability classification for graph products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, and for each v∈Γ let (M_v,φ_v) be a von Neumann algebra not equal to C with normal faithful state. Let M_Γ=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,φ_v). Then M_Γ is amenable if and only if both conditions hold: every vertex algebra M_v is amenable; and whenever distinct vertices v,w∈Γ do not share an edge, one has dim M_v=dim M_w=2 and Link_Γ({v,w})=Γ\{v,w}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this as Proposition 6.3 of Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson and does not reprove it. The quoted classification isolates the only way non-edge free-product behavior can remain amenable: both endpoint algebras must be two-dimensional and all remaining vertices must commute with both endpoints.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.9, diffuseness criterion under trace-zero vertex unitaries
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M_Γ,τ_Γ)=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,τ_v) be a graph product of tracial von Neumann algebras over a simple graph Γ. Assume that for every vertex v∈Γ there is a unitary u_v∈M_v with τ_v(u_v)=0. Then M_Γ is diffuse if either at least one vertex algebra M_v is diffuse, or Γ is not a complete graph.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this from Theorem E of Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson. The underlying mechanism is that trace-zero unitaries supply nontrivial reduced words; if the graph is not complete, alternating reduced words generate diffuse behavior even if all vertex algebras are finite-dimensional.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.2, in irreducible graphs normalizer non-intertwining is equivalent to algebra non-intertwining
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite irreducible simple graph. For each v∈Γ let M_v≠C be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful trace, and let M_Γ be the tracial graph product. Let N⊆M_Γ be a diffuse von Neumann subalgebra. Then the following are equivalent: N does not intertwine into M_{Γ_0} for any strict subgraph Γ_0⊊Γ; and Nor_{M_Γ}(N)′′ does not intertwine into M_{Γ_0} for any strict subgraph Γ_0⊊Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One direction is immediate because N⊆Nor_{M_Γ}(N)′′. Conversely, choose a subgraph Λ⊆Γ minimal with N≺M_Λ and N not≺M_{λ} for every strict subgraph λ⊊Λ. Since N is diffuse, Λ is nonempty. Apply Proposition 5.8 to the family of all strict subgraphs of Λ; it gives Nor_{M_Γ}(N)′′≺M_{Λ∪Link(Λ)}. If the normalizer does not intertwine into a strict subgraph of Γ, then Λ∪Link(Λ)=Γ. Irreducibility of Γ rules out a nontrivial decomposition Γ=Λ∪Link(Λ) with both parts nonempty, so Link(Λ) must be empty and hence Λ=Γ. Thus N itself cannot intertwine into any proper subgraph.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.3, prime-or-amenable dichotomy for irreducible graph products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite irreducible graph with |Γ|≥2. For each v∈Γ let M_v≠C be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful trace, and let M_Γ be the tracial graph product. Suppose M_Γ is a II_1 factor and M_Γ does not intertwine into M_{Γ_0} for any strict subgraph Γ_0⊊Γ. Then M_Γ is either prime or amenable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume M_Γ is not prime, so M_Γ=N_1⊗N_2 with diffuse factors N_1,N_2. Since Nor(N_1)′′=M_Γ and M_Γ avoids all proper subgraph algebras, Lemma 8.2 implies N_1 avoids all proper subgraph algebras. Choose a diffuse amenable von Neumann subalgebra A⊆N_2. Then Nor(A)′′ contains N_1, so Nor(A)′′ also avoids every proper subgraph algebra, and Lemma 8.2 implies A itself avoids every proper subgraph algebra. For each vertex v, use the amalgamated free product M_Γ=M_Star(v)∗_{M_Link(v)}M_{Γ\{v}} and apply Vaes's trichotomy to A. The alternatives A≺M_Link(v) and Nor(A)′′≺M_Star(v) or M_{Γ\{v}} are impossible because those are proper subgraph algebras. Hence Nor(A)′′ is amenable relative to M_Link(v) for every v. Repeatedly applying Theorem 5.3 over all vertices gives amenability relative to ⋂_v M_Link(v)=C, so Nor(A)′′ is amenable. Thus N_1 is amenable; by symmetry N_2 is amenable; hence M_Γ is amenable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.8, reduced-word conditional expectation vanishing
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, let Λ⊆Γ be a subgraph, and for each vertex v let (M_v,τ_v) be tracial. Let u∈W_Γ\W_Λ, where W_Γ is the right-angled Coxeter group indexing graph-product word types. Let v,v′∈W_Γ be such that every letter at the start of v, respectively v′, does not commute with any letter at the end of u^{-1}, respectively u. Let w,w′∈W_Γ satisfy |w|≤|v| and |w′|≤|v′|. Then for all a∈˚M_w, x∈˚M_{v^{-1} u v′}, and b∈˚M_{w′}, one has E_{M_Λ}(a x b)=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define H(u)=⊕_{s∈W(u)}˚H_s and M(u)=⊕_{s∈W(u)}˚M_s, where W(u) consists of words that remain reduced after left multiplication by u. If y_1∈M(u^{-1}), y_2∈˚M_u, and y_3∈M(u), then every reduced component of y_1* y_2 y_3 contains u as a subword; since u∉W_Λ, its expectation onto M_Λ is zero. Write x=x_1* x_2 x_3 with x_1∈˚M_v, x_2∈˚M_u, x_3∈˚M_{v′}. Using the creation-annihilation decomposition of graph-product left multiplication, Lemma 2.6 shows that x_3 b lies in H(u) because |w′|≤|v′| and the starting-letter noncommutation prevents cancellation of u; similarly x_1 a* lies in H(u^{-1}). Therefore a x b has the form y_1* y_2 y_3 with y_1∈M(u^{-1}), y_2∈˚M_u, y_3∈M(u), and its M_Λ-expectation is zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.10, trace-zero unitaries force M_Γ not to intertwine into any proper subgraph
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph with |Γ|≥3 and Star_Γ(v)≠Γ for every vertex v∈Γ. For each v∈Γ let (M_v,τ_v) be a tracial von Neumann algebra containing a unitary u_v with τ_v(u_v)=0. Then for every strict subgraph Λ⊊Γ, the graph product M_Γ does not intertwine into M_Λ inside M_Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix Λ⊊Γ and choose a vertex v∈Γ\Λ. Under |Γ|≥3 and Star(s)≠Γ for all s, the right-angled Coxeter group W_Γ is icc, so the conjugacy class of v is infinite. Choose elements v_n∈W_Γ with |v_n^{-1} v v_n|≥2n+1. By multiplying v_n on the left by initial letters commuting with v, one may assume every letter at the start of v_n does not commute with v; the conjugate v_n^{-1} v v_n remains unchanged and is reduced, and |v_n|≥n. If v_n^{-1} v v_n=s_1...s_l is reduced, define U_n=u_{s_1}...u_{s_l}∈˚M_{v_n^{-1} v v_n}; it is a unitary because the u_s are vertex unitaries and the word is reduced. For reduced coefficients a∈˚M_w and b∈˚M_{w′} with |w|,|w′|≤n, Lemma 8.8 gives E_{M_Λ}(a U_n b)=0. Approximate arbitrary x,y∈M_Γ in L^2 by finite sums of reduced words whose lengths are ≤n; for large n, the expectation E_{M_Λ}(x U_n y) is arbitrarily small in L^2. Hence ||E_{M_Λ}(x U_n y)||_2→0 for all x,y∈M_Γ, and Popa's criterion gives M_Γ not≺_{M_Γ}M_Λ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.4, primeness for graph products of II_1 factors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite simple graph with |Γ|≥2. For each v∈Γ let M_v be a II_1 factor with its trace. Then the tracial graph product M_Γ=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,τ_v) is prime if and only if Γ is irreducible, meaning that Γ is not a nontrivial join Γ=Γ_1∪Γ_2 with Γ_1,Γ_2 nonempty and Link_Γ(Γ_1)=Γ_2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Γ is irreducible, M_Γ is a factor by Caspers-Fima. Proposition 5.9 implies M_Γ cannot intertwine into any proper subgraph algebra, because any omitted vertex algebra is diffuse. Lemma 8.3 then gives that M_Γ is prime or amenable. Irreducibility with |Γ|≥2 makes Γ non-complete, and Proposition 7.8 rules out amenability for graph products of II_1 factors over such a graph. Hence M_Γ is prime. Conversely, if Γ is reducible, write Γ=Γ_1∪Γ_2 with Γ_1,Γ_2 nonempty and Link(Γ_1)=Γ_2. Then M_Γ=M_{Γ_1}⊗M_{Γ_2}, and each tensor factor is a diffuse II_1 factor, so M_Γ is not prime.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.11, prime factor result under trace-zero vertex unitaries
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be an irreducible finite simple graph with |Γ|≥3. For each v∈Γ let M_v≠C be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful trace τ_v, and assume M_v contains a unitary u_v with τ_v(u_v)=0. Then the tracial graph product M_Γ=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,τ_v) is a prime factor.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper first invokes Theorem E of Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson to conclude from the trace-zero unitary hypothesis and irreducibility that M_Γ is a II_1 factor. Since Γ is irreducible and |Γ|≥3, no vertex has Star(v)=Γ; otherwise Γ would split as {v} joined with Γ\{v}. Lemma 8.10 therefore gives M_Γ not≺M_Λ for every strict subgraph Λ⊊Γ. Lemma 8.3 implies M_Γ is prime or amenable. Finally, Proposition 7.8 rules out amenability: irreducibility with |Γ|≥3 excludes the exceptional amenable non-edge configuration Link({v,w})=Γ\{v,w}, and any nonamenable vertex would already rule out amenability. Thus M_Γ is prime.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.1, free-product decomposability exactly detects disconnected graphs for II_1 factor vertices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph with |Γ|≥2, and for each v∈Γ let M_v be a II_1 factor with separable predual and trace τ_v. Let M_Γ=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,τ_v). Then M_Γ can be decomposed as a tracial free product M_Γ=(M_1,τ_1)∗(M_2,τ_2) of II_1 factors if and only if Γ is not connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Γ is disconnected, any connected component Γ_0 gives M_Γ=M_{Γ_0}∗M_{Γ\Γ_0}. Conversely, suppose Γ is connected and M_Γ=M_1∗M_2. Fix v∈Γ and choose, by Ozawa-Popa, an amenable II_1 subfactor N_0⊆M_v with N_0′∩M_Γ=M_v′∩M_Γ=M_Link(v). Apply Vaes's trichotomy to the free product over C. The alternative N_0≺C is impossible because N_0 is diffuse. If Nor(N_0)′′≺M_i, the unitary-conjugacy method of Theorem 5.11 places N_0 into M_i; then Proposition 5.8 propagates this containment through normalizers along every edge path in the connected graph, forcing every vertex algebra M_w, and hence all of M_Γ, into a conjugate of M_i, contradiction. Thus Nor(N_0)′′ must be amenable. Since M_Link(v)⊆Nor(N_0)′′, every link algebra is amenable. Proposition 7.8 then implies each Link(v) is a clique and its vertex algebras are amenable. A connected graph whose every link is a clique is complete, so M_Γ is an amenable tensor product of amenable II_1 factors. But the hyperfinite II_1 factor cannot be a nontrivial free product of II_1 factors, giving the contradiction.

proof_techniques.name: Link-based amalgamated free product decomposition
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly decomposes a graph product around a vertex v as M_Γ=M_Star(v)∗_{M_Link(v)}M_{Γ\{v}}. This turns graph-product questions into amalgamated free product questions where Vaes's normalizer dichotomy applies: an amenable subalgebra either intertwines into the link algebra, has normalizer intertwining into one side, or has normalizer amenable relative to the link.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original irreducible graph, Star(v) is always proper when |Γ|≥3, so applying this decomposition at all vertices can force any amenability obstruction to be relative to all link algebras simultaneously. Combined with the intersection theorem, this may collapse relative amenability to amenability over C.

proof_techniques.name: Conditional-expectation calculus for graph-product reduced words
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 5.1 gives a precise formula for E_{M_{Γ_2}}(a*E_{M_{Γ_1}}(x)b) when a and b are reduced words decomposed into parts lying in Γ_1, Γ_2, and a middle part. The formula identifies the surviving component as an expectation onto Γ_1∩Γ_2∩Link(middle word), multiplied by a scalar inner product of the middle word components.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the main computational tool for proving vanishing of conditional expectations onto proper subgraph algebras. In the original trace-zero unitary setting, it underlies the construction of long reduced unitaries witnessing M_Γ not≺M_Λ for proper Λ.

proof_techniques.name: Long conjugate trace-zero unitaries
proof_techniques.description: In Lemma 8.10, trace-zero unitaries in the vertex algebras are multiplied along long reduced words v_n^{-1} v v_n in the right-angled Coxeter group W_Γ. These products are unitaries in M_Γ supported on reduced word spaces whose central subword contains a vertex outside a chosen proper subgraph Λ, so their conditional expectations onto M_Λ vanish against all bounded-length reduced coefficients.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique uses exactly the original hypothesis that every M_v has a trace-zero unitary. It supplies a concrete sequence or net of unitaries that can rule out embeddings into proper subgraph algebras, a likely ingredient in excluding join/tensor/amenability obstructions to proper proximality.

proof_techniques.name: Relative amenability intersection
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.3 shows that if a subalgebra P is amenable relative to two subgraph algebras M_{Γ_1} and M_{Γ_2}, then it is amenable relative to M_{Γ_1∩Γ_2}. The proof identifies the relevant Jones basic construction correspondence over the two subgraphs with a correspondence over the intersection using the conditional-expectation formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of proper proximality produces relative amenability to multiple links Link(v), this theorem can iterate over vertices. Since ⋂_v Link(v)=∅, one can often promote relative amenability to actual amenability, contradicting nonamenability.

proof_techniques.name: Quasi-normalizer localization by minimal subgraph support
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 5.8 says that if a subalgebra A lies in a subgraph algebra M_Λ but does not intertwine into specified smaller subgraph algebras, then its quasi-normalizer is forced into the enlarged subgraph Λ_emb built from Λ and appropriate link vertices. In the common minimal case where the smaller subgraphs are all proper subgraphs of Λ, this enlargement is Λ∪Link(Λ).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Irreducibility is precisely a condition ruling out nontrivial Λ∪Link(Λ)=Γ decompositions. Thus this localization can be used to show that large normalizers cannot be supported on proper subgraphs unless a join obstruction exists.

proof_techniques.name: Irreducibility as exclusion of join/tensor obstructions
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses irreducibility in the form: if Λ⊆Γ is nonempty and Λ∪Link(Λ)=Γ, then either Λ=Γ or Γ decomposes as a nontrivial join, contradicting irreducibility. This observation is central in Lemma 8.2 and in primeness arguments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Proper proximality is often obstructed by tensor product or join phenomena. The exact graph-theoretic irreducibility hypothesis in the original problem is designed to rule out these obstructions, and the paper provides a tested way to convert that into operator-algebraic non-intertwining.

proof_techniques.name: Normalizer propagation along graph paths
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 9.1, once a diffuse amenable subfactor of one vertex algebra is conjugated into one side of a free product decomposition, quasi-normalizer control forces adjacent vertex algebras into the same side. Repeating along paths propagates the containment to the whole connected component.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For connected components of the original graph, this provides a way to propagate local information from one vertex or link to all vertices. If proper proximality is first shown for components or for a subgraph, this technique may help extend it across connected paths.

proof_techniques.name: Using trace-zero unitaries for diffuseness, factoriality, and nonamenability inputs
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly invokes results from Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson showing that trace-zero vertex unitaries give strong structural consequences for graph products, including diffuseness criteria and, in Section 8, factoriality under the relevant irreducibility assumptions. Amenability is then ruled out using the graph-product amenability classification.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypotheses match this trace-zero unitary framework. Before proving proper proximality, one can likely use the paper's extracted consequences to reduce to a nonamenable II_1 factor with no proper-subgraph intertwining.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not prove proper proximality for general graph product von Neumann algebras. Its Theorem 8.11 nevertheless matches the original graph-theoretic and trace-zero-unitary hypotheses closely and proves that the corresponding graph product is a prime factor. Remark 9.5 cites Ding-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli [26, Proposition 3.7] for proper proximality of certain graph product groups, specifically examples ∗_{v,Γ_i}F_2 when Radius(Γ_i)≥3; this is only a related-work pointer in the paper, not a theorem proved there. Section 8 shows that the trace-zero unitary assumption is used concretely to build long reduced-word unitaries, not merely for abstract factoriality. Proposition 7.8 is useful for ruling out amenability: in an irreducible graph with at least three vertices, the exceptional amenable non-edge configuration Link({v,w})=Γ\{v,w} would be a join decomposition and is therefore excluded.
why_relevant: Develops rigidity results for von Neumann algebraic graph products and proves technical theorems on relative amenability and embeddings of quasi-normalizers. Those tools are relevant for controlling subgraph subalgebras and excluding tensor/join-type obstructions in irreducible graph products.
